name: barbell deadlift
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and the barbell on the ground in front of you.
Bend your knees and hinge at the hips to lower your torso and grip the barbell with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your back straight and chest lifted as you drive through your heels to lift the barbell off the ground, extending your hips and knees.
As you stand up straight, squeeze your glutes and keep your core engaged.
Lower the barbell back down to the ground by bending at the hips and knees, keeping your back straight.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and the barbell on the ground in front of you. Bend your knees and hinge at the hips to lower your torso and grip the barbell with an overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Keep your back straight and chest lifted as you drive through your heels to lift the barbell off the ground, extending your hips and knees. As you stand up straight, squeeze your glutes and keep your core engaged. Lower the barbell back down to the ground by bending at the hips and knees, keeping your back straight. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Axial loading under fatigue may worsen asymmetrical curves. Prefer split-stance or supported variations.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Loaded spinal flexion or heavy axial loading should be avoided. Use supported variations.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
